responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity infrastructure faces a structural dependency on Apple and Google platform integrity signals, creating supplier concentration risk and strategic autonomy loss. The core tension is between maintaining MitID's conservative, certification-heavy trust model and establishing platform-neutral resilience before identity-stack lock-in hardens irreversibly. The project navigates this by shifting intervention from retrofitting MitID (impossible) toward influencing AltID procurement and EU standards before decisions crystallize. Success requires balancing technical credibility (demonstrators), political leverage (coalition and framing), regulatory alignment (eIDAS/NSIS), and realistic acceptance of institutional inertia. The five levers below address the critical trade-offs: technical pathway design (feasibility vs. disruption), stakeholder engagement strategy (insider vs. external pressure), demonstrator scope (lean vs. feature-rich), procurement intervention timing (early vs. late), and EU alignment (national vs. transnational). These levers control whether the project influences AltID design before hardening occurs, whether technical evidence convinces skeptics without triggering defensive resistance, and whether framing shifts public discourse from ideology to resilience.

levers.name: Technical-Pathway Neutrality Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Choosing between standards-based abstraction vs. platform-proprietary workarounds affects demonstrator credibility with both security experts and procurement officials. Systemic: A pure FIDO2/OpenID Connect approach reduces platform-specific code by ~70% and improves portability signals, lowering AltID architect skepticism; conversely, incorporating selective Apple/Google APIs gains faster proof-of-concept but signals technical capitulation to incumbent lock-in, reducing political leverage by ~40%. Strategic: Early pathway selection locks downstream architecture; a conservative, maximally-portable design enables EU alignment and standards participation; a hybrid approach secures faster momentum but weakens the core sovereignty argument and complicates procurement language.
options: Pure standards-based architecture (FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect) with zero Apple/Google platform-integrity dependencies, accepting longer timeline and compatibility edge cases
Hybrid approach using standards-compliant flows as primary path but incorporating optional Apple/Google attestation signals for fallback resilience testing, balancing speed and independence

Agile multi-implementation strategy deploying three parallel minimal demos (one pure standards, one Apple-optimized, one Google-optimized) to empirically demonstrate equivalence and guide procurement language
levers.review_lever: Controls Technical Purity vs. Political Pragmatism. Weakness: The options do not adequately address the risk that demonstrators achieving feature-parity with MitID through standards alone may require security-review timelines that extend beyond AltID procurement windows, potentially rendering technical evidence irrelevant if decisions have already hardened.

levers.name: Stakeholder-Coalition Sequencing Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Coalition-building order (civil society first vs. Digitaliseringsstyrelsen first vs. MitID operator first) determines which actors perceive the project as external pressure vs. internal risk-mitigation. Systemic: Beginning with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and academic credibility (~60% institutional receptiveness) establishes insider legitimacy; leading with privacy advocates and Folketinget alignment creates external pressure (~45% institutional defensiveness). Strategic: Early insider engagement risks capture by incumbent framing; external pressure risks being dismissed as ideological; sequencing determines whether AltID architects view platform-neutrality requirements as co-created solutions or externally-imposed constraints affecting design agency and adoption likelihood.
options: Inside-first sequencing: Engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and AltID design teams from month 1 as research partners, framing the project as institutional resilience planning and de-risking, reducing external visibility until phase two deliverables create technical evidence

Outside-in coalition mobilization: Activate civil society, academic networks, and Folketinget digital-policy committee members first, building external consensus on supplier concentration risk before formal engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, increasing public framing momentum but risking defensive institutional response

Parallel dual-track engagement: Simultaneously develop insider relationships with sympathetic Digitaliseringsstyrelsen staff and EU digital identity working groups while cultivating external coalition visibility through policy briefs and media engagement, accepting higher coordination complexity for doubled leverage pathways
levers.review_lever: Controls Institutional Buy-In vs. External Pressure Leverage. Weakness: All three options assume that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen decision-makers can be influenced by phase-three evidence, but do not account for scenario where AltID procurement timelines or political priorities shift independently of the project's coalition-building pace, rendering sequencing choices irrelevant if institutional windows close prematurely.

levers.name: Demonstrator-Scope Discipline Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Lean single-path demonstrator (GrapheneOS + WebAuthn only) completes in 12–14 weeks with ~1.2M DKK; feature-rich multi-platform reference implementation requires 18–22 weeks and ~2.8M DKK. Systemic: Lean scope prioritizes time-to-evidence and focus, reducing scope creep and budget risk by ~60%; feature-rich scope builds deeper technical credibility and competitive pressure on AltID architects (~35% influence lift) but consumes contingency reserves and delays policy engagement by 8–10 weeks. Strategic: Demonstrator must ship before AltID design decisions (estimated month 16–18 window); lean demos unlock earlier coalition-building and standards-engagement; feature-rich demos risk project becoming a product development effort rather than a policy-intervention mechanism.
options: Minimalist single-stack demonstrator: One end-to-end flow (GrapheneOS authentication against simulated Danish IdP using FIDO2 + OpenID Connect) achieving ~40% architectural coverage, completing month 12, leaving 4 months for policy acceleration and EU engagement

Dual-demonstrator lean approach: Mobile-oriented flow plus browser-based fallback path, each addressing core supplier-concentration scenarios with ~60% architectural coverage per demo, target completion month 14, enabling parallel procurement consultation

Reference-grade reference implementation: Three full-stack demonstrators (privacy-respecting Android, hardware token, browser-based) with production-grade documentation, security testing, and interoperability reports, achieving ~85% architectural credibility but requiring month 18–20 delivery and consuming phase-two budget reserves
levers.review_lever: Controls Evidence Credibility vs. Timeline-to-Impact. Weakness: The options fail to address the risk that a minimalist demonstrator, while fast, may be dismissed by AltID architects as insufficient proof of production-grade feasibility, particularly if security testing reports are light or lack external certification, undermining rather than supporting policy arguments.

levers.name: Procurement-Intervention Timing and Intensity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Early intervention (month 12, draft AltID RFP phase) requires complete phase-two deliverables and creates dependency on procurement timeline predictability; late intervention (month 18–20, final design) works with mature technical evidence but faces entrenched architect decisions and higher change-friction. Systemic: Early intervention targeting procurement language drafts can influence 60–70% of architectural assumptions; late intervention focused on vendor-selection criteria influences ~25–35% of final choices. Strategic: Premature engagement without demonstrators risks being perceived as uninformed advocacy; delayed engagement sacrifices influence for credibility but may arrive after design lock-in, making the intervention symbolic rather than structural.
options: Aggressive early leverage: Deliver phase-two outputs by month 14 and request formal AltID design-review consultation immediately, proposing specific procurement RFP language on platform neutrality and fallback authentication, accepting risk of perceived premature technical maturity claims

Calibrated staged intervention: Phase-two completion (month 16) followed by two-month AltID RFP comment window participation, submitting procurement guidance tied to published demonstrators and feasibility report, balancing credibility and timeliness

Deferred standards-pressure model: Treat Danish AltID as secondary; focus phase-three effort on EU Digital Identity Wallet standards bodies and international eIDAS2 implementation guidance, allowing Denmark to adopt EU-harmonized supplier-neutrality language rather than leading domestic procurement fights
levers.review_lever: Controls Domestic Procurement Influence vs. EU-Level Standards Leverage. Weakness: The aggressive early-leverage and calibrated-staged options both assume AltID procurement teams will actively solicit external input during design phases, but do not account for institutional practice where identity-infrastructure design occurs in closed working groups with limited external consultation windows, potentially rendering intervention timing irrelevant regardless of demonstrator maturity.

levers.name: Resilience-Framing and Risk-Articulation Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Security-and-sovereignty framing emphasizes geopolitical and compliance risk (eIDAS, NSIS, digital autonomy); resilience-and-contingency framing emphasizes service continuity and supplier-concentration risk; privacy framing emphasizes data minimization and platform opacity. Systemic: Resilience framing generates ~55% receptiveness among procurement and continuity-planning officials; sovereignty framing drives ~70% alignment with Folketinget and EU policy circles but ~30% resistance from incumbent operators citing ideological overtones; privacy framing activates civil society but ~40% effectiveness with procurement stakeholders treating it as secondary. Strategic: Frame selection shapes which audiences perceive platform-neutrality requirements as essential infrastructure vs. ideological constraint vs. privacy hygiene, determining whether AltID architects adopt requirements voluntarily or defend against them as external pressure.
options: Resilience-and-continuity-first framing: Lead all policy communication with supplier-concentration risk, service-fallback necessity, and geopolitical contingency planning, positioning platform neutrality as operational continuity design rather than ideology or regulation-avoidance, prioritizing procurement and official risk-mitigation language

Layered frame-stacking approach: Open with resilience framing for institutional audiences, add sovereignty and digital-autonomy language for political constituencies, and background privacy and democratic-accountability angles for civil-society coalition, adjusting emphasis per stakeholder segment and phase timing

Privacy-and-data-sovereignty integration: Position platform-neutral authentication as essential to preventing Apple and Google from systematizing telemetry and behavior tracking embedded in MitID verification flows, framing reduced lock-in as human-rights and democratic-integrity infrastructure, targeting civil society and EU fundamental-rights constituencies
levers.review_lever: Controls Institutional Receptiveness vs. Ideological Clarity. Weakness: The resilience-first and layered frame-stacking approaches assume that procurement officials will distinguish between genuine continuity risk and policy advocacy, but do not account for scenario where any external pressure on procurement decisions—regardless of framing—triggers defensive institutional resistance, making frame selection less decisive than stakeholder perception of external vs. internal legitimacy.

levers.name: Technical-Team Composition and Resilience Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Specialist-heavy team (dedicated mobile-security lead, dedicated policy specialist) completes deliverables faster (~15% timeline compression) with deeper domain expertise; distributed generalist team (multi-skilled individuals, rotating roles) requires longer ramp-up (~8–10 week contingency) but eliminates single-points-of-failure and improves knowledge retention. Systemic: Specialist team produces higher-caliber technical reports and demonstrators (~40% external-credibility lift) but creates staffing risk if key personnel depart (team collapse risk ~35% if not backfilled); generalist team achieves ~25% credibility via broader perspectives and cross-validation but requires more internal coordination and may miss deep technical edge cases (~20% technical-debt risk). Strategic: Team structure choice determines whether project survives key-person departure, whether knowledge transfers to Danish institutions post-project, and whether deliverables remain institution-independent or become dependent on ongoing consultant relationships.
options: Lean specialist core: 1 lead researcher (mobile security), 1 regulatory specialist, 1 policy coordinator, 1 technical demonstrator lead, rotating junior contractor; deep expertise, faster output, explicit key-person contingency planning with backup hire triggers

Distributed peer-review model: 2–3 mid-level security engineers (no single lead), 1 policy specialist paired with rotating Digitaliseringsstyrelsen embedded liaison, 1 technical coordinator managing demonstrator work distributed across multiple contractors, emphasizing knowledge distribution and institutional knowledge transfer

Hybrid embedded model: Specialist core (lead researcher, policy specialist, technical lead) paired with 2–3 full-time fellows embedded from Danish universities or security-qualified R&D facilities, rotating annually, building institutional capacity and knowledge retention within Denmark while maintaining external expertise lines
levers.review_lever: Controls Expertise Depth vs. Organizational Resilience. Weakness: All three options assume sufficient budget flexibility to hire and retain mid-to-senior talent in Copenhagen's tight labor market, but do not adequately account for scenario where specialized expertise (e.g., mobile-security researchers with eIDAS background) is unavailable or cost-prohibitive locally, forcing dependence on remote contractors whose time-zone misalignment and lower institutional integration reduce coordination effectiveness and deliverable quality.

responses.summary: The five levers are well-positioned to address the project's core strategic tensions: technical credibility vs. political leverage, institutional buy-in vs. external pressure, speed vs. scope, intervention timing vs. impact, and framing resonance vs. clarity. However, one critical dimension is underexplored: the project lacks a contingency-decision framework for scenario where AltID procurement is delayed, cancelled, or conducted entirely outside formal public-consultation windows. Add to Lever 4 (Procurement-Intervention Timing) a fourth option: "Institutional-absorption pathway: If formal AltID RFP engagement fails or procurement occurs in closed process, redirect phase-three effort toward formal adoption of project findings into Danish digital-identity standards bodies (NSIS implementation working groups, government IT security architecture reviews), creating institutional precedent for platform-neutral requirements in future procurement cycles, extending influence horizon beyond single AltID project." This addresses the realistic scenario outlined in the plan where institutional inertia and incumbent resistance may block direct procurement intervention, while preserving project value through standards and institutional-knowledge channels.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's platform-neutrality project faces a second layer of critical decisions beyond technical and stakeholder dimensions: how to structure institutional accountability, regulatory alignment, budget-contingency management, and external-validation mechanisms. The core tension shifts from whether change is possible to how to ensure it persists despite institutional inertia, budget constraints, and shifting political priorities. These five levers address: (1) regulatory alignment with eIDAS2 and NSIS to create binding requirements rather than voluntary guidance; (2) third-party validation strategy to build external credibility against incumbent skepticism; (3) budget-trigger and phase-gate discipline to prevent scope creep and misallocation; (4) Danish institutional capacity-building to reduce post-project dependence on external consultants; and (5) EU-standards coordination to amplify Danish voice through transnational mechanisms. Together, these levers determine whether the project's outputs become institutionalized policy anchors or remain isolated research artifacts.

levers.name: Regulatory-Alignment and Standards-Formalization Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Aligning demonstrators and policy proposals to explicit eIDAS2 Article 6a (interoperability) and NSIS Level Substantial requirements creates binding reference points; generic resilience arguments lack regulatory teeth and are treated as policy preferences. Systemic: Formal eIDAS2/NSIS alignment enables procurement language to cite EU regulatory obligations (~65% enforceability lift); generic framing allows vendors to claim compliance variance and exemptions (~15% enforceability). Mapping project findings to specific regulatory articles (e.g., "platform-neutral fallback satisfies eIDAS2 portability obligations") adds ~50M DKK regulatory-compliance risk weight to incumbent vendor proposals, shifting cost-benefit calculus. Strategic: Early regulatory grounding makes platform-neutrality a compliance requirement rather than a preference; late regulatory linking appears opportunistic and weakens standards-authority partnership needed for EU-level amplification.
options: Early regulatory integration: From phase one, embed eIDAS2 and NSIS compliance specialists in feasibility work, explicitly mapping demonstrator architectures and policy proposals to specific regulatory articles, publishing regulatory alignment as primary deliverable alongside technical report, positioning project as compliance-enablement mechanism

Regulatory-annotation approach: Complete technical and policy work independently in phases one and two, then commission formal legal review (month 15–17) mapping findings to eIDAS2, NSIS, and relevant Danish law (Databeskyttelsesloven), attaching regulatory documentation to AltID procurement submissions without retrofitting prior work

Transnational regulatory co-development: Coordinate with EU Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and national competent authorities from phase one onward, co-authoring implementation guidance documents that embed platform-neutrality requirements directly into EU regulatory interpretation, allowing Denmark to cite emerging EU consensus rather than independent advocacy
levers.review_lever: Controls Regulatory Binding Power vs. Project Independence. Weakness: Early regulatory integration risks creating dependence on regulatory-authority decision-making timelines beyond project control; if eIDAS2 implementation deadlines slip or regulatory guidance is delayed, project's regulatory credibility advantage evaporates, leaving technical demonstrators unsupported by formal compliance pressure.

levers.name: External-Validation and Third-Party Certification Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Commissioning independent security assessment by internationally-recognized audit firm (e.g., Cure53, Trail of Bits) costs ~1.5M DKK but validates demonstrators without inherent-bias perception; internal security review costs ~0.3M DKK but is dismissed by vendors as self-validation. Systemic: Third-party security certification adds ~45% credibility lift with AltID architects and ~60% credibility with EU regulatory bodies; peer-review by academic security researchers adds ~35% credibility but lacks procurement-authority weight. Strategic: Certification also functions as external veto mechanism; if audit uncovers fundamental flaws, project must pivot or absorb reputational damage; conversely, clean bill of health from recognized auditor becomes procurement-language anchor that vendors struggle to dispute.
options: Independent Big Four audit engagement: Commission formal security assessment from recognized international firm with EU regulatory expertise, targeting month 15 delivery, publishing unredacted report (with vendor NDAs only for operational secrets), creating third-party validation that procurement and regulatory authorities treat as authoritative standard

Academic and open-source validation model: Conduct security reviews through university cryptography labs and open-source security communities (e.g., OpenSSF, Tor Project), publishing findings through peer-reviewed channels and technical conferences, building credibility with academic stakeholders and EU standards bodies but accepting lower procurement-authority weight

Dual-validation approach: Commission lightweight independent assessment (~0.6M DKK) focusing on architectural risk and compliance gaps, supplemented by open academic review of cryptographic and standards-compliance choices, balancing audit-authority weight with academic-research transparency and cost efficiency
levers.review_lever: Controls Audit Authority vs. Project Autonomy and Cost. Weakness: Big Four audit engagement creates dependency on auditor's regulatory interpretation and may produce recommendations (e.g., "fallback authentication requires hardware tokens only") that conflict with project's lean-demonstrator strategy, forcing choice between audit credibility and technical pragmatism.

levers.name: Budget-Gate Discipline and Contingency-Reallocation Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict phase-gate enforcement (phase-two funding released only if phase-one delivers published report + institutional engagement) prevents budget drift; flexible contingency model (pools 15–20% unallocated reserves) enables rapid pivoting if procurement windows close or stakeholders shift. Systemic: Gate discipline reduces budget overrun risk by ~70% but forces hard delivery commitments and may prevent exploring unexpected opportunities; flexible reserves enable opportunistic engagement (e.g., impromptu EU working-group participation) but create ~35% scope-creep risk and reduce accountability clarity. Strategic: Budget structure choice determines whether project operates as managed-risk institutional initiative (gates) or entrepreneurial-opportunity-capture vehicle (flexibility), affecting credibility with both conservative procurement officials (preferring gates) and coalition partners (preferring adaptability).
options: Hard phase-gating with binary go/no-go decisions: Phase two funds released only if phase one produces published report, formal institutional engagement, and documented AltID engagement plan; phase three only if phase two delivers two functional demonstrators and updated procurement roadmap; unspent allocations revert to sponsor, enforcing delivery discipline and reducing scope creep

Adaptive milestone-and-reserve model: Allocate 70% of budget to committed phase milestones, retain 30% as strategic reserve accessible via monthly steering-committee approval for emerging opportunities (EU working-group involvement, accelerated stakeholder engagement, extended security review), balancing accountability with agility

Conditional reallocation framework: Phase-gate releases as primary discipline, but allow cross-phase reallocation within strict envelope (e.g., reallocate up to 0.8M DKK from phase three to phase two if demonstrator work requires extended security hardening), with quarterly sponsor review and published rationale for all reallocations, maintaining transparency and accountability while preserving tactical flexibility
levers.review_lever: Controls Budget Accountability vs. Strategic Agility. Weakness: Hard phase-gating may force phase-one to produce premature institutional engagement (e.g., early meeting with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen) to unlock phase-two funding, regardless of readiness or relationship maturity, creating low-quality engagement that damages rather than advances coalition-building.

levers.name: Danish-Institutional-Capacity Transfer and Post-Project Sustainability Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Embedding knowledge within Danish institutions (university research groups, government digital-policy offices, security-qualified R&D centers) costs ~1.8M DKK (25% budget premium) but ensures findings persist post-project; external-consultant delivery model costs ~0.4M DKK less but creates dependency on consultant re-engagement for follow-on work. Systemic: Embedded capacity enables ~70% of phase-three procurement engagement to occur through Danish staff (lower political friction than external actors); consultant-dependent model requires ~60% of phase-three work to flow through external advisors, reducing local ownership and increasing stakeholder skepticism (~35% institutional-receptiveness penalty). Strategic: Capacity-building determines whether AltID procurement teams view platform-neutrality requirements as externally-imposed vs. locally-understood strategic imperatives; institutional transfer also increases likelihood that findings influence 2026–2029 digital-strategy implementation and subsequent identity-infrastructure decisions.
options: Full embedded-fellow model: Hire 2–3 full-time fellows (months 1–24) from Danish universities or government agencies, rotating annually, embedding project work within institutional structures, publishing findings under Danish institutional authors, establishing permanent research groups focused on platform-neutral identity infrastructure post-project

Hybrid capacity-transfer approach: Maintain lean external core team, but contract 1–2 part-time embedded researchers from target institutions (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, university security labs, government CIO office) for 12+ months, rotating roles (design review, procurement consultation, standards participation), creating institutional touchpoints without full employment overhead

Consultant-to-institution transition model: Execute project primarily through external consultants (phases one and two), then redirect phase-three effort toward formalizing findings into Danish institution (e.g., establish "Platform Sovereignty Research Group" at Copenhagen Business School or Danish Technical University), transferring consultant knowledge to permanent academic position, creating legacy within Danish higher education and digital-policy infrastructure
levers.review_lever: Controls Post-Project Institutional Persistence vs. Budget Efficiency. Weakness: Full embedded-fellow model creates organizational overhead and requires finding qualified Danish researchers with simultaneous expertise in mobile security, digital identity, and EU regulatory frameworks—a scarce talent pool—risking high hiring costs or extended vacancy periods that delay project work.

levers.name: EU-Standards Amplification and Transnational-Coalition Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Engaging EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and ETSI standards bodies from phase one requires hiring policy staff with EU-working-group experience (~0.5M DKK annual cost) and accepts lower Danish-procurement focus; domestic-first strategy prioritizes Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID engagement (lower overhead, faster policy traction). Systemic: Early EU engagement amplifies Danish voice through transnational mechanisms (~60% amplification in EU regulatory interpretation) but dilutes focus on domestic AltID timeline; EU work also creates ~4–8 month policy-cycle delays (EU working-group meeting schedules, consensus timelines). Strategic: EU amplification creates long-term policy binding power (platform-neutrality requirements embedded in European Digital Identity Wallet framework benefit all EU member states) but risks missing Danish domestic intervention window if AltID decisions harden during EU-engagement ramp-up.
options: EU-first transnational strategy: Allocate phase-one effort primarily to identifying sympathetic EU member-state delegations (Netherlands, Austria, Germany) and EU standards bodies, co-authoring implementation guidance on platform-neutral authentication with transnational coalition, allowing Denmark to join broader EU consensus rather than leading domestic fight, deferring Danish procurement engagement until EU guidance solidifies (month 18–20)

Parallel domestic-and-EU dual-track approach: Simultaneously pursue AltID engagement (targeting month 14–16 RFP input) and EU Digital Identity Wallet working-group participation, synchronizing policy messaging across channels, with EU work reinforcing Danish procurement leverage by citing emerging transnational consensus, accepting higher coordination complexity

Domestic-first with selective EU leverage: Prioritize Danish AltID procurement intervention (months 12–18), then redirect phase-three effort toward EU standards bodies, using successful Danish case study as template for European guidance, positioning Denmark as implementation pioneer rather than follower, accepting lower EU-process influence but maximizing domestic policy impact
levers.review_lever: Controls European Policy Leverage vs. Domestic Procurement Timeliness. Weakness: The EU-first and parallel dual-track approaches both assume EU working-group processes will accelerate or align with Danish procurement timelines, but EU consensus-building typically requires 18–24 month cycles; if AltID procurement decisions harden before EU guidance emerges, EU engagement becomes post-hoc legitimation rather than decision-shaping mechanism.

responses.summary: These five levers are substantially different from the first set, addressing institutional sustainability, regulatory power, external validation, budget discipline, and transnational coordination rather than technical design and stakeholder sequencing. However, the set has one significant gap: it underestimates the risk of incumbent vendor and Apple/Google organizational responses. Add a sixth lever addressing vendor-response management: "Incumbent-Response Anticipation and Counter-Messaging Model" that forecasts vendor defensive strategies (FUD campaigns, technical counter-claims, compliance-equivalence arguments) and develops pre-emptive rebuttals grounded in project deliverables. Specifically, structure phase-two demonstrator work and external validation to directly address the most predictable vendor objections (e.g., "platform-neutral authentication cannot achieve MitID's anti-fraud performance"), publishing technical rebuttals alongside audit reports. This transforms external validation from passive credibility-building to active vendor-response management, increasing AltID architects' confidence that adopting platform-neutrality requirements will survive vendor pressure campaigns.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's platform-neutrality project must now address a third critical dimension: how to sustain political momentum across changing administrations, manage media and public narrative, protect against geopolitical pressure, ensure technical work remains operationally independent, and build contractual/IP structures that lock in commitments beyond the 24-month project window. These five levers address: (1) political-continuity mechanisms to insulate platform-neutrality requirements from electoral cycles; (2) media-narrative management to preempt incumbent counter-messaging and shape public discourse; (3) geopolitical-resilience planning to protect the project and its outputs if US-Denmark relations or EU-US tensions affect platform-policy coordination; (4) technical independence and operational security to prevent Apple/Google interference or surveillance of demonstrator research; and (5) contractual and IP-ownership structures that embed platform-neutrality language in AltID procurement documents permanently. Together, these levers ensure that even if political will weakens or external pressure intensifies, the project's core outputs become institutionalized constraints on future digital-identity architecture.

levers.name: Political-Continuity and Cross-Party Institutionalization Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Securing written cross-party parliamentary support (all-party digital-sovereignty working group, formal Folketinget resolution) for platform-neutrality principles creates political inertia beyond electoral cycles; single-party or ministry-level backing is vulnerable to government change (~65% risk of policy reversal if administration changes). Systemic: All-party institutionalization increases platform-neutrality survival probability across election cycles by ~75%; ministry-level support alone drops survival probability to ~40% if new government assigns digital portfolio to less-sympathetic minister. Multi-year parliamentary working group creates formal review mechanism for AltID and subsequent identity projects, embedding supplier-neutrality checks into future procurement cycles. Strategic: Cross-party lock-in requires early (phase one) parliamentary engagement and careful framing avoiding partisan positioning; partisan framing undermines institutionalization and triggers political opposition.
options: Cross-party parliamentary working group: Establish formal Folketinget committee (months 2–4) including members from Socialdemokraterne, Venstre, De Radikale, and other parties, tasking committee with annual review of platform-dependency risks and supplier-concentration in digital identity, creating parliamentary anchor that survives government turnover and embedding platform-neutrality into formal digital-strategy governance

Single-party strategic positioning: Secure formal endorsement from governing-coalition parties (months 1–3) for platform-neutrality language in coalition agreement or digital-strategy roadmap, maximizing immediate ministry-level traction but accepting ~60% risk of policy reversal if coalition composition changes in 2027 or 2028 election

Ministry-and-institution dual-anchor approach: Secure written commitments from both Digitaliseringsstyrelsen leadership and Rigsrevisionens audit mandate (National Audit Office) to include supplier-concentration and platform-dependency risk in annual government-IT audits (2026–2030), creating dual institutional scrutiny that persists across government changes and parliamentary compositions, reducing single-point-of-failure risk
levers.review_lever: Controls Electoral Durability vs. Near-Term Political Leverage. Weakness: All-party working-group establishment requires months of consensus-building before formal launch, delaying substantive parliamentary engagement until month 4–6; if AltID procurement is accelerated or conducted before working group matures, early parliamentary positioning advantage is lost regardless of eventual cross-party institutionalization.

levers.name: Media-Narrative and Counter-Messaging Management Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Proactive media strategy (phase-one policy briefings, opinion pieces in Weekendavisen and Jyllands-Posten, researcher interviews) shapes public discourse on supplier concentration before Apple/Google launch defensive narratives; reactive media strategy (responding to vendor claims after phase-two demonstrators) cedes narrative frame (~50% reduction in public resonance). Systemic: Early narrative control establishes "digital sovereignty" and "service resilience" as default public frames (~65% media adoption); reactive response allows vendors to frame platform-neutrality as "ideological attack on innovation" (~55% media amplification of vendor narrative). Media narrative also influences parliamentarian perception; early positive coverage increases cross-party receptiveness by ~40%; late or defensive coverage reduces policy uptake by ~30%. Strategic: Media strategy timing and tone choice determine whether AltID procurement teams face public pressure to adopt platform-neutrality (early strategy) or can defend exclusivity as technical inevitability (reactive strategy).
options: Early proactive narrative campaign: Launch phase-one policy communications immediately (month 2–3) via academic researchers, digital-rights organizations, and sympathetic journalists, establishing "digital sovereignty" and "service continuity" framings in public discourse before vendor counter-messaging, publishing op-eds in major Danish outlets and coordinating international coverage through EU digital-rights networks

Embedded researcher visibility model: Maintain lower public profile during phases one and two, but secure speaking opportunities at technical conferences (IEEE Security & Privacy, USENIX Security), academic journals, and EU digital-identity forums, building credibility with technical and policy elites while avoiding triggering early vendor defensive responses or political backlash

Counter-narrative rapid-response framework: Monitor media coverage and vendor communications (months 1–24), maintaining rapid-response capability to correct technical misrepresentations and vendor FUD through letters to editors, technical blog posts, and researcher interviews, preserving narrative corrections without presuming early media dominance
levers.review_lever: Controls Narrative Dominance vs. Institutional Relationships. Weakness: Proactive narrative campaigns risk antagonizing Digitaliseringsstyrelsen leadership and AltID architects who perceive media pressure as disrespectful to internal decision-making authority; aggressive media strategy may trigger institutional defensive posturing that hardens positions against platform-neutrality requirements rather than persuading through policy evidence.

levers.name: Geopolitical-Resilience and US-Denmark Pressure-Mitigation Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: If US administration exerts pressure on Denmark via NATO, trade, or diplomatic channels to avoid restrictions on Apple/Google platform integrity (framed as national-security interoperability necessity), project faces potential government reversal. Systemic: Framing platform-neutrality as EU regulatory obligation (eIDAS2 compliance) rather than unilateral Danish choice reduces US leverage (~60% resistance to external pressure); grounding requirements in international standards (OpenID Foundation, FIDO Alliance, ETSI) distributes ownership beyond Danish discretion (~55% diplomatic insulation). Strategic: Heavy US pressure scenario (low probability but high impact) requires project to anchor platform-neutrality in transnational and EU structures before US concerns escalate; if anchoring occurs late (phase three), project becomes vulnerable to transatlantic diplomatic pressure.
options: EU-and-standards-first security positioning: From phase one, frame platform-neutrality as mandatory eIDAS2 compliance and alignment with FIDO Alliance and OpenID Foundation international standards, positioning Denmark as implementing EU and international requirements rather than independent policy choice, reducing bilateral US-Denmark friction and making US pressure diplomatically counterproductive

Transatlantic intelligence and diplomatic preparation: Engage Danish Foreign Ministry (month 3–4) to conduct informal diplomatic soundings with US State Department and NSA regarding platform-neutrality requirements, securing early signaling of US concerns (or absence thereof) and developing counter-messaging if pressure emerges, enabling proactive diplomatic positioning rather than reactive crisis response

National-security and intelligence-community partnership: Secure endorsement from Danish military cybersecurity authorities (Forsvarets Efterretningstjeneste, Center for Cybersecurity) framing platform-neutrality as essential to preventing Apple/Google platform-intelligence dependencies that could be exploited by foreign adversaries, converting platform-neutrality from regulatory-compliance issue to national-security imperative that aligns with US intelligence interests rather than conflicting with them
levers.review_lever: Controls Geopolitical Insulation vs. Bilateral Relationships. Weakness: Framing platform-neutrality as NATO and national-security imperative may inadvertently invite US government negotiation or coordination proposals (e.g., "we will limit data sharing if you align with our preferred platform integrity standards"), converting sovereignty argument into leverage for new forms of US pressure rather than achieving genuine independence.

levers.name: Technical-Operational Independence and Research-Security Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict operational security (air-gapped demonstrator development environments, restricted CI/CD pipelines, internal-only code repositories until publication) costs ~0.8M DKK and adds 4–6 week timeline friction; standard industry DevOps practices (GitHub, public CI/CD, open repositories during development) reduce costs by ~0.2M DKK but allow Apple/Google or other actors to monitor demonstrator design decisions in real-time. Systemic: Operational security prevents demonstrators from becoming intelligence-gathering targets for platform vendors (~70% protection gain); open development allows vendors to preemptively design counter-measures or file blocking patents (~40% risk to demonstrator uniqueness and credibility). Strategic: Operational independence also signals to AltID architects that research is not influenced by platform-vendor feedback or pressure; conversely, transparent open-source development builds community trust and peer review but sacrifices tactical surprise and creates vulnerability to coordinated vendor opposition.
options: Secure-enclave development model: Conduct all demonstrator development within access-controlled Copenhagen facility (university computer lab or security-qualified R&D center), using air-gapped development machines, restricted Git repositories with encryption, and no cloud CI/CD systems; publish only final demonstrator code and security assessments post-delivery (month 16–18), preventing real-time vendor monitoring but accepting reduced open-source community participation during development

Open-source development with security compartmentalization: Maintain public GitHub repositories and transparent CI/CD for demonstrator code, but partition sensitive components (e.g., cryptographic key management, eIDAS regulatory-compliance mappings) into private branches with restricted committer access, balancing community engagement and peer review with operational security for vendor-sensitive elements

Graduated-disclosure security model: Develop demonstrators in secure environments (months 1–14), then conduct controlled beta-release to vetted security researchers and academic reviewers (month 15), publishing full code and audit reports (month 16) only after independent security assessment is complete and vendors have insufficient time to develop counter-measures before AltID procurement consultation begins
levers.review_lever: Controls Research Security vs. Community Engagement and Transparency. Weakness: Secure-enclave and graduated-disclosure models reduce transparency and may trigger criticism from open-source communities and digital-rights advocates who expect publicly-funded research to be immediately open; this creates coalition-relationship tension where privacy and security fundamentalists question project's commitment to transparency, undermining credibility with some stakeholder groups.

levers.name: Contractual-Lock-In and Procurement-Mandate Embedding Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Including explicit platform-neutrality language in AltID procurement RFP ("Vendors must provide fallback authentication path independent of Apple App Store and Google Play") creates binding requirement enforceable through vendor selection criteria; vague guidance ("consider platform-neutral alternatives") allows procurement teams to treat supplier neutrality as optional consideration. Systemic: Binding procurement language locks platform-neutrality into all AltID-derived identity systems for ~10+ year lifecycle (typical government IT contracts); optional guidance permits AltID vendor to propose Apple/Google-exclusive solutions that meet specification (~65% risk that vendors claim compliance despite no fallback path). Strategic: Contractual embedding requires phase-three success in influencing RFP language; if AltID RFP is finalized before month 18, binding language opportunity is lost and subsequent correction requires expensive contract modification or new procurement cycle.
options: Mandatory-requirement procurement language: Work with AltID procurement teams (months 14–18) to embed platform-neutrality as explicit MUST-HAVE requirement in RFP, e.g., "Fallback authentication path must not depend exclusively on Apple App Store, Google Play, or proprietary platform-integrity APIs; vendor must demonstrate compliant implementation using standards such as FIDO2 and OpenID Connect," making platform-neutrality a vendor-selection veto criterion and contract-performance requirement enforceable through penalty clauses

Preferred-solution scoring approach: Propose platform-neutrality as a high-scoring evaluation criterion in AltID RFP (e.g., "15% of vendor-evaluation score awarded for demonstrating platform-neutral fallback capability"), allowing vendors to propose Apple/Google-exclusive solutions but creating financial incentive for compliant architectures; accepting lower enforcement power but increasing vendor adoption likelihood and reducing procurement resistance

Phased-compliance contractual pathway: Structure AltID procurement to require platform-neutral fallback deployment in phase 2 (months 24–36 of contract), allowing initial launch on Apple/Google-exclusive path but contractually binding vendor to deliver neutral alternative within defined timeframe, reducing upfront resistance while ensuring long-term supplier-concentration reduction and creating leverage for post-launch iteration
levers.review_lever: Controls Binding Enforcement Power vs. Vendor-Adoption Feasibility. Weakness: Mandatory-requirement language may cause qualified vendors to exclude themselves from AltID bidding (interpreting platform-neutrality as architecturally infeasible or commercially unviable), reducing bidder competition and increasing procurement costs or extending vendor-selection timelines, potentially delaying AltID delivery beyond government digital-strategy deadlines and triggering administrative pressure to relax requirements.

responses.summary: These five levers address political durability, narrative control, geopolitical insulation, research security, and contractual permanence—dimensions largely absent from the first two lever sets. The set is well-balanced across stakeholder pressure points and time horizons. However, one critical gap remains: the project lacks a clear mechanism for managing technical debt and post-project maintenance of demonstrators and policy artifacts. Add a sixth lever addressing post-project sustainability and technical governance: "Demonstrator-Stewardship and Open-Source-Governance Model" that specifies whether demonstrators transition to permanent open-source projects (requiring ongoing maintenance funding and governance structure), are frozen as historical artifacts (accepted obsolescence post-project), or are transferred to permanent institutional stewardship (university or government research group). Specifically, commit phase-three resources to establishing governance charters, maintenance roadmaps, and funding mechanisms (e.g., annual contribution budgets from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or EU digital-identity funding) that keep demonstrators and audit reports current through 2029, ensuring they remain credible reference implementations as AltID and subsequent identity-infrastructure projects iterate, rather than becoming dated and irrelevant within 2–3 years post-launch.

levers.name: Technical-Pathway Neutrality Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Choosing between standards-based abstraction vs. platform-proprietary workarounds affects demonstrator credibility with both security experts and procurement officials. Systemic: A pure FIDO2/OpenID Connect approach reduces platform-specific code by ~70% and improves portability signals, lowering AltID architect skepticism; conversely, incorporating selective Apple/Google APIs gains faster proof-of-concept but signals technical capitulation to incumbent lock-in, reducing political leverage by ~40%. Strategic: Early pathway selection locks downstream architecture; a conservative, maximally-portable design enables EU alignment and standards participation; a hybrid approach secures faster momentum but weakens the core sovereignty argument and complicates procurement language.
options: Pure standards-based architecture (FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect) with zero Apple/Google platform-integrity dependencies, accepting longer timeline and compatibility edge cases
Hybrid approach using standards-compliant flows as primary path but incorporating optional Apple/Google attestation signals for fallback resilience testing, balancing speed and independence

Agile multi-implementation strategy deploying three parallel minimal demos (one pure standards, one Apple-optimized, one Google-optimized) to empirically demonstrate equivalence and guide procurement language
levers.review: Controls Technical Purity vs. Political Pragmatism. Weakness: The options do not adequately address the risk that demonstrators achieving feature-parity with MitID through standards alone may require security-review timelines that extend beyond AltID procurement windows, potentially rendering technical evidence irrelevant if decisions have already hardened.

levers.name: Stakeholder-Coalition Sequencing Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Coalition-building order (civil society first vs. Digitaliseringsstyrelsen first vs. MitID operator first) determines which actors perceive the project as external pressure vs. internal risk-mitigation. Systemic: Beginning with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and academic credibility (~60% institutional receptiveness) establishes insider legitimacy; leading with privacy advocates and Folketinget alignment creates external pressure (~45% institutional defensiveness). Strategic: Early insider engagement risks capture by incumbent framing; external pressure risks being dismissed as ideological; sequencing determines whether AltID architects view platform-neutrality requirements as co-created solutions or externally-imposed constraints affecting design agency and adoption likelihood.
options: Inside-first sequencing: Engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and AltID design teams from month 1 as research partners, framing the project as institutional resilience planning and de-risking, reducing external visibility until phase two deliverables create technical evidence

Outside-in coalition mobilization: Activate civil society, academic networks, and Folketinget digital-policy committee members first, building external consensus on supplier concentration risk before formal engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, increasing public framing momentum but risking defensive institutional response

Parallel dual-track engagement: Simultaneously develop insider relationships with sympathetic Digitaliseringsstyrelsen staff and EU digital identity working groups while cultivating external coalition visibility through policy briefs and media engagement, accepting higher coordination complexity for doubled leverage pathways
levers.review: Controls Institutional Buy-In vs. External Pressure Leverage. Weakness: All three options assume that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen decision-makers can be influenced by phase-three evidence, but do not account for scenario where AltID procurement timelines or political priorities shift independently of the project's coalition-building pace, rendering sequencing choices irrelevant if institutional windows close prematurely.

levers.name: Demonstrator-Scope Discipline Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Lean single-path demonstrator (GrapheneOS + WebAuthn only) completes in 12–14 weeks with ~1.2M DKK; feature-rich multi-platform reference implementation requires 18–22 weeks and ~2.8M DKK. Systemic: Lean scope prioritizes time-to-evidence and focus, reducing scope creep and budget risk by ~60%; feature-rich scope builds deeper technical credibility and competitive pressure on AltID architects (~35% influence lift) but consumes contingency reserves and delays policy engagement by 8–10 weeks. Strategic: Demonstrator must ship before AltID design decisions (estimated month 16–18 window); lean demos unlock earlier coalition-building and standards-engagement; feature-rich demos risk project becoming a product development effort rather than a policy-intervention mechanism.
options: Minimalist single-stack demonstrator: One end-to-end flow (GrapheneOS authentication against simulated Danish IdP using FIDO2 + OpenID Connect) achieving ~40% architectural coverage, completing month 12, leaving 4 months for policy acceleration and EU engagement

Dual-demonstrator lean approach: Mobile-oriented flow plus browser-based fallback path, each addressing core supplier-concentration scenarios with ~60% architectural coverage per demo, target completion month 14, enabling parallel procurement consultation

Reference-grade reference implementation: Three full-stack demonstrators (privacy-respecting Android, hardware token, browser-based) with production-grade documentation, security testing, and interoperability reports, achieving ~85% architectural credibility but requiring month 18–20 delivery and consuming phase-two budget reserves
levers.review: Controls Evidence Credibility vs. Timeline-to-Impact. Weakness: The options fail to address the risk that a minimalist demonstrator, while fast, may be dismissed by AltID architects as insufficient proof of production-grade feasibility, particularly if security testing reports are light or lack external certification, undermining rather than supporting policy arguments.

levers.name: Procurement-Intervention Timing and Intensity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Early intervention (month 12, draft AltID RFP phase) requires complete phase-two deliverables and creates dependency on procurement timeline predictability; late intervention (month 18–20, final design) works with mature technical evidence but faces entrenched architect decisions and higher change-friction. Systemic: Early intervention targeting procurement language drafts can influence 60–70% of architectural assumptions; late intervention focused on vendor-selection criteria influences ~25–35% of final choices. Strategic: Premature engagement without demonstrators risks being perceived as uninformed advocacy; delayed engagement sacrifices influence for credibility but may arrive after design lock-in, making the intervention symbolic rather than structural.
options: Aggressive early leverage: Deliver phase-two outputs by month 14 and request formal AltID design-review consultation immediately, proposing specific procurement RFP language on platform neutrality and fallback authentication, accepting risk of perceived premature technical maturity claims

Calibrated staged intervention: Phase-two completion (month 16) followed by two-month AltID RFP comment window participation, submitting procurement guidance tied to published demonstrators and feasibility report, balancing credibility and timeliness

Deferred standards-pressure model: Treat Danish AltID as secondary; focus phase-three effort on EU Digital Identity Wallet standards bodies and international eIDAS2 implementation guidance, allowing Denmark to adopt EU-harmonized supplier-neutrality language rather than leading domestic procurement fights
levers.review: Controls Domestic Procurement Influence vs. EU-Level Standards Leverage. Weakness: The aggressive early-leverage and calibrated-staged options both assume AltID procurement teams will actively solicit external input during design phases, but do not account for institutional practice where identity-infrastructure design occurs in closed working groups with limited external consultation windows, potentially rendering intervention timing irrelevant regardless of demonstrator maturity.

levers.name: Resilience-Framing and Risk-Articulation Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Security-and-sovereignty framing emphasizes geopolitical and compliance risk (eIDAS, NSIS, digital autonomy); resilience-and-contingency framing emphasizes service continuity and supplier-concentration risk; privacy framing emphasizes data minimization and platform opacity. Systemic: Resilience framing generates ~55% receptiveness among procurement and continuity-planning officials; sovereignty framing drives ~70% alignment with Folketinget and EU policy circles but ~30% resistance from incumbent operators citing ideological overtones; privacy framing activates civil society but ~40% effectiveness with procurement stakeholders treating it as secondary. Strategic: Frame selection shapes which audiences perceive platform-neutrality requirements as essential infrastructure vs. ideological constraint vs. privacy hygiene, determining whether AltID architects adopt requirements voluntarily or defend against them as external pressure.
options: Resilience-and-continuity-first framing: Lead all policy communication with supplier-concentration risk, service-fallback necessity, and geopolitical contingency planning, positioning platform neutrality as operational continuity design rather than ideology or regulation-avoidance, prioritizing procurement and official risk-mitigation language

Layered frame-stacking approach: Open with resilience framing for institutional audiences, add sovereignty and digital-autonomy language for political constituencies, and background privacy and democratic-accountability angles for civil-society coalition, adjusting emphasis per stakeholder segment and phase timing

Privacy-and-data-sovereignty integration: Position platform-neutral authentication as essential to preventing Apple and Google from systematizing telemetry and behavior tracking embedded in MitID verification flows, framing reduced lock-in as human-rights and democratic-integrity infrastructure, targeting civil society and EU fundamental-rights constituencies
levers.review: Controls Institutional Receptiveness vs. Ideological Clarity. Weakness: The resilience-first and layered frame-stacking approaches assume that procurement officials will distinguish between genuine continuity risk and policy advocacy, but do not account for scenario where any external pressure on procurement decisions—regardless of framing—triggers defensive institutional resistance, making frame selection less decisive than stakeholder perception of external vs. internal legitimacy.

levers.name: Technical-Team Composition and Resilience Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Specialist-heavy team (dedicated mobile-security lead, dedicated policy specialist) completes deliverables faster (~15% timeline compression) with deeper domain expertise; distributed generalist team (multi-skilled individuals, rotating roles) requires longer ramp-up (~8–10 week contingency) but eliminates single-points-of-failure and improves knowledge retention. Systemic: Specialist team produces higher-caliber technical reports and demonstrators (~40% external-credibility lift) but creates staffing risk if key personnel depart (team collapse risk ~35% if not backfilled); generalist team achieves ~25% credibility via broader perspectives and cross-validation but requires more internal coordination and may miss deep technical edge cases (~20% technical-debt risk). Strategic: Team structure choice determines whether project survives key-person departure, whether knowledge transfers to Danish institutions post-project, and whether deliverables remain institution-independent or become dependent on ongoing consultant relationships.
options: Lean specialist core: 1 lead researcher (mobile security), 1 regulatory specialist, 1 policy coordinator, 1 technical demonstrator lead, rotating junior contractor; deep expertise, faster output, explicit key-person contingency planning with backup hire triggers

Distributed peer-review model: 2–3 mid-level security engineers (no single lead), 1 policy specialist paired with rotating Digitaliseringsstyrelsen embedded liaison, 1 technical coordinator managing demonstrator work distributed across multiple contractors, emphasizing knowledge distribution and institutional knowledge transfer

Hybrid embedded model: Specialist core (lead researcher, policy specialist, technical lead) paired with 2–3 full-time fellows embedded from Danish universities or security-qualified R&D facilities, rotating annually, building institutional capacity and knowledge retention within Denmark while maintaining external expertise lines
levers.review: Controls Expertise Depth vs. Organizational Resilience. Weakness: All three options assume sufficient budget flexibility to hire and retain mid-to-senior talent in Copenhagen's tight labor market, but do not adequately account for scenario where specialized expertise (e.g., mobile-security researchers with eIDAS background) is unavailable or cost-prohibitive locally, forcing dependence on remote contractors whose time-zone misalignment and lower institutional integration reduce coordination effectiveness and deliverable quality.

levers.name: Regulatory-Alignment and Standards-Formalization Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Aligning demonstrators and policy proposals to explicit eIDAS2 Article 6a (interoperability) and NSIS Level Substantial requirements creates binding reference points; generic resilience arguments lack regulatory teeth and are treated as policy preferences. Systemic: Formal eIDAS2/NSIS alignment enables procurement language to cite EU regulatory obligations (~65% enforceability lift); generic framing allows vendors to claim compliance variance and exemptions (~15% enforceability). Mapping project findings to specific regulatory articles (e.g., "platform-neutral fallback satisfies eIDAS2 portability obligations") adds ~50M DKK regulatory-compliance risk weight to incumbent vendor proposals, shifting cost-benefit calculus. Strategic: Early regulatory grounding makes platform-neutrality a compliance requirement rather than a preference; late regulatory linking appears opportunistic and weakens standards-authority partnership needed for EU-level amplification.
options: Early regulatory integration: From phase one, embed eIDAS2 and NSIS compliance specialists in feasibility work, explicitly mapping demonstrator architectures and policy proposals to specific regulatory articles, publishing regulatory alignment as primary deliverable alongside technical report, positioning project as compliance-enablement mechanism

Regulatory-annotation approach: Complete technical and policy work independently in phases one and two, then commission formal legal review (month 15–17) mapping findings to eIDAS2, NSIS, and relevant Danish law (Databeskyttelsesloven), attaching regulatory documentation to AltID procurement submissions without retrofitting prior work

Transnational regulatory co-development: Coordinate with EU Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and national competent authorities from phase one onward, co-authoring implementation guidance documents that embed platform-neutrality requirements directly into EU regulatory interpretation, allowing Denmark to cite emerging EU consensus rather than independent advocacy
levers.review: Controls Regulatory Binding Power vs. Project Independence. Weakness: Early regulatory integration risks creating dependence on regulatory-authority decision-making timelines beyond project control; if eIDAS2 implementation deadlines slip or regulatory guidance is delayed, project's regulatory credibility advantage evaporates, leaving technical demonstrators unsupported by formal compliance pressure.

levers.name: External-Validation and Third-Party Certification Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Commissioning independent security assessment by internationally-recognized audit firm (e.g., Cure53, Trail of Bits) costs ~1.5M DKK but validates demonstrators without inherent-bias perception; internal security review costs ~0.3M DKK but is dismissed by vendors as self-validation. Systemic: Third-party security certification adds ~45% credibility lift with AltID architects and ~60% credibility with EU regulatory bodies; peer-review by academic security researchers adds ~35% credibility but lacks procurement-authority weight. Strategic: Certification also functions as external veto mechanism; if audit uncovers fundamental flaws, project must pivot or absorb reputational damage; conversely, clean bill of health from recognized auditor becomes procurement-language anchor that vendors struggle to dispute.
options: Independent Big Four audit engagement: Commission formal security assessment from recognized international firm with EU regulatory expertise, targeting month 15 delivery, publishing unredacted report (with vendor NDAs only for operational secrets), creating third-party validation that procurement and regulatory authorities treat as authoritative standard

Academic and open-source validation model: Conduct security reviews through university cryptography labs and open-source security communities (e.g., OpenSSF, Tor Project), publishing findings through peer-reviewed channels and technical conferences, building credibility with academic stakeholders and EU standards bodies but accepting lower procurement-authority weight

Dual-validation approach: Commission lightweight independent assessment (~0.6M DKK) focusing on architectural risk and compliance gaps, supplemented by open academic review of cryptographic and standards-compliance choices, balancing audit-authority weight with academic-research transparency and cost efficiency
levers.review: Controls Audit Authority vs. Project Autonomy and Cost. Weakness: Big Four audit engagement creates dependency on auditor's regulatory interpretation and may produce recommendations (e.g., "fallback authentication requires hardware tokens only") that conflict with project's lean-demonstrator strategy, forcing choice between audit credibility and technical pragmatism.

levers.name: Budget-Gate Discipline and Contingency-Reallocation Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict phase-gate enforcement (phase-two funding released only if phase-one delivers published report + institutional engagement) prevents budget drift; flexible contingency model (pools 15–20% unallocated reserves) enables rapid pivoting if procurement windows close or stakeholders shift. Systemic: Gate discipline reduces budget overrun risk by ~70% but forces hard delivery commitments and may prevent exploring unexpected opportunities; flexible reserves enable opportunistic engagement (e.g., impromptu EU working-group participation) but create ~35% scope-creep risk and reduce accountability clarity. Strategic: Budget structure choice determines whether project operates as managed-risk institutional initiative (gates) or entrepreneurial-opportunity-capture vehicle (flexibility), affecting credibility with both conservative procurement officials (preferring gates) and coalition partners (preferring adaptability).
options: Hard phase-gating with binary go/no-go decisions: Phase two funds released only if phase one produces published report, formal institutional engagement, and documented AltID engagement plan; phase three only if phase two delivers two functional demonstrators and updated procurement roadmap; unspent allocations revert to sponsor, enforcing delivery discipline and reducing scope creep

Adaptive milestone-and-reserve model: Allocate 70% of budget to committed phase milestones, retain 30% as strategic reserve accessible via monthly steering-committee approval for emerging opportunities (EU working-group involvement, accelerated stakeholder engagement, extended security review), balancing accountability with agility

Conditional reallocation framework: Phase-gate releases as primary discipline, but allow cross-phase reallocation within strict envelope (e.g., reallocate up to 0.8M DKK from phase three to phase two if demonstrator work requires extended security hardening), with quarterly sponsor review and published rationale for all reallocations, maintaining transparency and accountability while preserving tactical flexibility
levers.review: Controls Budget Accountability vs. Strategic Agility. Weakness: Hard phase-gating may force phase-one to produce premature institutional engagement (e.g., early meeting with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen) to unlock phase-two funding, regardless of readiness or relationship maturity, creating low-quality engagement that damages rather than advances coalition-building.

levers.name: Danish-Institutional-Capacity Transfer and Post-Project Sustainability Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Embedding knowledge within Danish institutions (university research groups, government digital-policy offices, security-qualified R&D centers) costs ~1.8M DKK (25% budget premium) but ensures findings persist post-project; external-consultant delivery model costs ~0.4M DKK less but creates dependency on consultant re-engagement for follow-on work. Systemic: Embedded capacity enables ~70% of phase-three procurement engagement to occur through Danish staff (lower political friction than external actors); consultant-dependent model requires ~60% of phase-three work to flow through external advisors, reducing local ownership and increasing stakeholder skepticism (~35% institutional-receptiveness penalty). Strategic: Capacity-building determines whether AltID procurement teams view platform-neutrality requirements as externally-imposed vs. locally-understood strategic imperatives; institutional transfer also increases likelihood that findings influence 2026–2029 digital-strategy implementation and subsequent identity-infrastructure decisions.
options: Full embedded-fellow model: Hire 2–3 full-time fellows (months 1–24) from Danish universities or government agencies, rotating annually, embedding project work within institutional structures, publishing findings under Danish institutional authors, establishing permanent research groups focused on platform-neutral identity infrastructure post-project

Hybrid capacity-transfer approach: Maintain lean external core team, but contract 1–2 part-time embedded researchers from target institutions (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, university security labs, government CIO office) for 12+ months, rotating roles (design review, procurement consultation, standards participation), creating institutional touchpoints without full employment overhead

Consultant-to-institution transition model: Execute project primarily through external consultants (phases one and two), then redirect phase-three effort toward formalizing findings into Danish institution (e.g., establish "Platform Sovereignty Research Group" at Copenhagen Business School or Danish Technical University), transferring consultant knowledge to permanent academic position, creating legacy within Danish higher education and digital-policy infrastructure
levers.review: Controls Post-Project Institutional Persistence vs. Budget Efficiency. Weakness: Full embedded-fellow model creates organizational overhead and requires finding qualified Danish researchers with simultaneous expertise in mobile security, digital identity, and EU regulatory frameworks—a scarce talent pool—risking high hiring costs or extended vacancy periods that delay project work.

levers.name: EU-Standards Amplification and Transnational-Coalition Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Engaging EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and ETSI standards bodies from phase one requires hiring policy staff with EU-working-group experience (~0.5M DKK annual cost) and accepts lower Danish-procurement focus; domestic-first strategy prioritizes Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID engagement (lower overhead, faster policy traction). Systemic: Early EU engagement amplifies Danish voice through transnational mechanisms (~60% amplification in EU regulatory interpretation) but dilutes focus on domestic AltID timeline; EU work also creates ~4–8 month policy-cycle delays (EU working-group meeting schedules, consensus timelines). Strategic: EU amplification creates long-term policy binding power (platform-neutrality requirements embedded in European Digital Identity Wallet framework benefit all EU member states) but risks missing Danish domestic intervention window if AltID decisions harden during EU-engagement ramp-up.
options: EU-first transnational strategy: Allocate phase-one effort primarily to identifying sympathetic EU member-state delegations (Netherlands, Austria, Germany) and EU standards bodies, co-authoring implementation guidance on platform-neutral authentication with transnational coalition, allowing Denmark to join broader EU consensus rather than leading domestic fight, deferring Danish procurement engagement until EU guidance solidifies (month 18–20)

Parallel domestic-and-EU dual-track approach: Simultaneously pursue AltID engagement (targeting month 14–16 RFP input) and EU Digital Identity Wallet working-group participation, synchronizing policy messaging across channels, with EU work reinforcing Danish procurement leverage by citing emerging transnational consensus, accepting higher coordination complexity

Domestic-first with selective EU leverage: Prioritize Danish AltID procurement intervention (months 12–18), then redirect phase-three effort toward EU standards bodies, using successful Danish case study as template for European guidance, positioning Denmark as implementation pioneer rather than follower, accepting lower EU-process influence but maximizing domestic policy impact
levers.review: Controls European Policy Leverage vs. Domestic Procurement Timeliness. Weakness: The EU-first and parallel dual-track approaches both assume EU working-group processes will accelerate or align with Danish procurement timelines, but EU consensus-building typically requires 18–24 month cycles; if AltID procurement decisions harden before EU guidance emerges, EU engagement becomes post-hoc legitimation rather than decision-shaping mechanism.

levers.name: Political-Continuity and Cross-Party Institutionalization Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Securing written cross-party parliamentary support (all-party digital-sovereignty working group, formal Folketinget resolution) for platform-neutrality principles creates political inertia beyond electoral cycles; single-party or ministry-level backing is vulnerable to government change (~65% risk of policy reversal if administration changes). Systemic: All-party institutionalization increases platform-neutrality survival probability across election cycles by ~75%; ministry-level support alone drops survival probability to ~40% if new government assigns digital portfolio to less-sympathetic minister. Multi-year parliamentary working group creates formal review mechanism for AltID and subsequent identity projects, embedding supplier-neutrality checks into future procurement cycles. Strategic: Cross-party lock-in requires early (phase one) parliamentary engagement and careful framing avoiding partisan positioning; partisan framing undermines institutionalization and triggers political opposition.
options: Cross-party parliamentary working group: Establish formal Folketinget committee (months 2–4) including members from Socialdemokraterne, Venstre, De Radikale, and other parties, tasking committee with annual review of platform-dependency risks and supplier-concentration in digital identity, creating parliamentary anchor that survives government turnover and embedding platform-neutrality into formal digital-strategy governance

Single-party strategic positioning: Secure formal endorsement from governing-coalition parties (months 1–3) for platform-neutrality language in coalition agreement or digital-strategy roadmap, maximizing immediate ministry-level traction but accepting ~60% risk of policy reversal if coalition composition changes in 2027 or 2028 election

Ministry-and-institution dual-anchor approach: Secure written commitments from both Digitaliseringsstyrelsen leadership and Rigsrevisionens audit mandate (National Audit Office) to include supplier-concentration and platform-dependency risk in annual government-IT audits (2026–2030), creating dual institutional scrutiny that persists across government changes and parliamentary compositions, reducing single-point-of-failure risk
levers.review: Controls Electoral Durability vs. Near-Term Political Leverage. Weakness: All-party working-group establishment requires months of consensus-building before formal launch, delaying substantive parliamentary engagement until month 4–6; if AltID procurement is accelerated or conducted before working group matures, early parliamentary positioning advantage is lost regardless of eventual cross-party institutionalization.

levers.name: Media-Narrative and Counter-Messaging Management Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Proactive media strategy (phase-one policy briefings, opinion pieces in Weekendavisen and Jyllands-Posten, researcher interviews) shapes public discourse on supplier concentration before Apple/Google launch defensive narratives; reactive media strategy (responding to vendor claims after phase-two demonstrators) cedes narrative frame (~50% reduction in public resonance). Systemic: Early narrative control establishes "digital sovereignty" and "service resilience" as default public frames (~65% media adoption); reactive response allows vendors to frame platform-neutrality as "ideological attack on innovation" (~55% media amplification of vendor narrative). Media narrative also influences parliamentarian perception; early positive coverage increases cross-party receptiveness by ~40%; late or defensive coverage reduces policy uptake by ~30%. Strategic: Media strategy timing and tone choice determine whether AltID procurement teams face public pressure to adopt platform-neutrality (early strategy) or can defend exclusivity as technical inevitability (reactive strategy).
options: Early proactive narrative campaign: Launch phase-one policy communications immediately (month 2–3) via academic researchers, digital-rights organizations, and sympathetic journalists, establishing "digital sovereignty" and "service continuity" framings in public discourse before vendor counter-messaging, publishing op-eds in major Danish outlets and coordinating international coverage through EU digital-rights networks
